The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy parallel to the external iliac vessel, noting that the vessel has no branches in the pelvis except for the inferior epigastric artery, which is related to the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon attempts a lymphadenectomy, focusing on opening the barorectal space to access the parapsychological space. The ureter is identified, which is crucial for locating the uterine artery. The transverse view of the barorectal space is visible, resembling a bubble cone shape.

so this is very important and then the ureter in front of the uterine artery above the uterine artery is the deep uterine veins so this point is very important because if there is bleeding intra-op during the radical hysterectomy you have to tell the source of bleeding what will you do what will you do with the arterial venous you will tighten the ureter because if it comes from below it is a deep uterine vein and of course this is related to the ribocastric vessel we will explain it later or above it with a uterine artery.

The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy, carefully identifying the ureter. The ureter is visible with its mesentery, and the surgeon ensures to stay away from the mesenteric subline to prevent postoperative hydroureter.

The surgeon begins the pelvic lymphadenectomy, focusing on the lymphatic structures running along the veins and ligaments, including the cervix, iliac nodes, uterus, paravortic nodes, ovaries, and vagina. The iliopterator nerve is visible during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the boundaries of the internal iliac artery medially and the genitofemoral nerve laterally, positioned on the muscles. The dissection progresses towards the bladder, highlighting its significant anatomical role in the procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the dissection up to the interureteric orifices on both sides, highlighting the trigonal area and cautioning not to go under it.

The surgeon approaches the fascia of the rectum, carefully avoiding the rectal fat, which is visible and belongs to the rectum. The dissection maintains proximity to the back of the uterus, revealing the two layers of the rectum. The procedure begins with the creation of the colbutum.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to perform colpotomy with the colpotomizer visible in the surgical field. The ureteric anatomy is highlighted, showing the ureteric tunnel as a condensation of the pelvic fascia and a vascular strand of endo-pelvic fascia.
The surgeon performs a closure of the vaginal cuff using hexacorporeal sutures, demonstrating the technique visually.
The surgeon opens the pararectal space, followed by the paravesical space, taking anatomical considerations into account as the ureter originates medially from the promontory and enters the pelvis.
The video shows the final appearance of the pelvic region following pelvic lymphadenectomy and radical hysterectomy, with surgical specimens prepared for histological examination.
